exemplar.title: How does the vertical terminal velocity (m/s) of a marble (of mass 0.052kg) vary with the temperature of syrup (20.5-60Â°C) when dropped from a constant height into a container?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the vertical terminal velocity (m/s) of a marble (of mass 0.052kg) vary with the temperature of syrup (20.5-60Â°C) when dropped from a constant height into a container?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

files.fileName: Jack FalkestrÃ¶m IB Diploma - IA Lab report physics - FA 2025.pdf

strengths: Data presented in complete tables with units, uncertainties and properly labeled axes.
Graphs include clear titles, axis labels and error bars, supporting visual clarity.
Uncertainty calculations via the range method are shown, demonstrating rigor in measured averages.
Transparency in data processing by distinguishing measured and extrapolated values.
weaknesses: Omission of pixelâtoâmeter calibration procedure and its uncertainty in video analysis.
Inconsistent notation between v_r and v_t that may confuse interpretation.
No propagation of uncertainties through extrapolated data or linear regression parameters.
Blank cells for some extrapolated data hinder clarity on data completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The data analysis is mostly clear and precise in communication (band 3â4) but has significant omissions in uncertainty consideration and some inconsistencies (band 3â4). While tables and figures are wellâpresented, the lack of calibration details and uncertainty propagation limits the methodological robustness. A balanced score of 4/6 reflects strong presentation tempered by these notable gaps.

strengths: Research question embedded in a specific, authentic context with clear variables (mass, temperature range, fluid).
Detailed, numbered method steps and apparatus list enabling reproducibility with few ambiguities.
Clear derivation of theoretical relationships linking weight and drag for terminal velocity.
Wellâtabulated control variables and justification for experimental controls.
weaknesses: No calibration procedure described for the temperature probe or viscosity measurement method.
Ambiguous exponent notation in the theoretical relationship needs clarification.
Limited discussion of broader applications beyond the Olympic inspiration.
Sample size justification and quantitative treatment of drag coefficient assumption are underdeveloped.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research design scores solidly in band 3â4. The context is specific and appropriate (band 5â6), and the procedural description allows reproduction with few ambiguities (band 3â4). Methodological considerations are described but not fully explained (band 3â4). Balancing the clear context and replicability against the missing calibration details and incomplete justification, a holistic score of 4/6 accurately reflects the strengths and areas needing refinement.

strengths: Conclusion states the observed trend of increasing terminal velocity with temperature and cites numerical fit values.
Relevant reference to theoretical expectations and recognition of deviation from the original squared hypothesis.
Some comparison to accepted scientific context (densityâtemperature relationship and viscosity effects).
weaknesses: Inconsistency between concluding support for the squared hypothesis and the observed linear trend.
Limited depth in scientific context comparison; lacks quantitative alignment with Stokesâ law.
Conclusion does not fully reconcile hypothesis with results, causing confusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and largely consistent with the data but contains contradictory statements and only some contextual comparison (band 3â4). It does not fully justify or align the hypothesis, nor deeply compare to established models. A midâband score of 3/6 accurately captures this balanced performance.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (refraction, frame rate, extrapolation) and discusses their qualitative impact.
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (different tank geometry, higherâspeed camera, finer temperature intervals).
Reflects critical thinking by linking each suggested improvement to potential error reduction.
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative estimation of the magnitude of identified errors (e.g., refraction distortion).
Improvements are described but not explained in terms of how much they would improve precision or validity.
Does not specify how to implement and validate suggested refinements (e.g., calibration tests).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation describes specific limitations and realistic improvements, aligning with band 3â4, but remains qualitative without quantitative impact analysis (band 3â4). The reflections demonstrate critical awareness yet fall short of detailed explanation of how improvements would reduce uncertainties. A holistic score of 3/6 reflects this level of evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated, narrowly focused (terminal velocity of a 0.052 kg marble in syrup 20.5â60 Â°C) and embedded in an authentic scenario (inspired by Olympic swimming, then refined to viscosity effects). The student explicitly specifies mass, size, fluid, temperature range and method, giving the reader an immediate, tangible context for why the investigation matters and how it will be executed. This meets the description of âspecific and appropriate contextâ, although a brief relevance to wider physics applications could deepen it, justifying a 5 rather than 6.

Random uncertainties for temperature and velocity are stated; error bars plotted; range method explained. However, no propagation to derived quantities, no discussion of systematic errors in the analysis phase, and extrapolated points lack quantitative uncertainty treatment. This shows âevidence â¦ with some significant omissionsâ, matching band 3-4 but nearer the lower end.

Methodological considerations are present (mass, diameter, drop height controlled; justification for slow-motion, three trials, 5 Â°C steps, use of extrapolation noted). The student explains why each control matters but does not fully justify sample size, deal with refraction quantitatively, or ensure sufficient frames at higher temperatures. The considerations are described rather than thoroughly explained, so the strand sits comfortably in the middle of band 3-4; a score of 3 reflects the partial, not comprehensive, explanation.

The evaluation section identifies specific weaknesses: refraction in cylindrical beaker, clear marbles, 5 Â°C steps, limited frame rate, extrapolation error. The student comments on how each could distort results, albeit qualitatively. This description plus brief impact discussion fulfils âdescribes specific weaknessesâ, lightly touching on impact, so upper band 3-4 is appropriate.

The numbered method, apparatus list and schematic provide enough information for another student to replicate the set-up (drop height, temperature intervals, slow-motion filming, Tracker analysis). However, some ambiguities remain: syrup volume is given but not viscosity measurement procedure; the criteria for selecting the eight âpeak velocityâ points are vague; calibration of the temperature probe is omitted. Hence reproduction would be possible with âfew ambiguities,â aligning with band 3-4; detail is not exhaustive enough for the top band.

Processing includes averaging, uncertainty calculation, linear regression and RÂ². Yet extrapolated values replace missing data without a formal model, and no statistical test or propagation is performed. Some inconsistencies in velocity units across trials persist. Hence âsome significant omissionsâ, band 3-4, score 3.

Improvements are realistic and relevant (use glass cuboid tank, coloured marbles, higher-speed camera, finer temperature intervals, different fluid). They are described but not fully explained in terms of how much they would reduce uncertainty or improve validity. This fits ârealistic and relevant improvements are describedâ, earning a mid-band 3-4 score of 3.

Data tables are complete with units, uncertainties and scientific notation; graphs are titled, axes labelled and include error bars. Notation is sometimes inconsistent (v_r vs v_t) and the table formatting shows empty cells, but the overall presentation is clear and mostly precise, satisfying the âeither clear or preciseâ descriptor and edging toward both. Thus band 3-4, upper end.

There is a meaningful comparison to accepted physics: densityâtemperature link, Stokesâ law vs drag equation, discussion of viscosity. The comparison is correct but not deeply analysed or quantitatively checked, so it meets âsome relevant comparisonâ, band 3-4, score 3.

The student states that terminal velocity increases with temperature, quoting specific values and referencing the linear fit and RÂ². The conclusion is largely consistent with processed data but wavers when claiming it âsupportsâ a squared hypothesis then refutes it. Overall it is relevant and mostly supported, fitting band 3-4, lower end.

annotations.comment: Control variables are tabulated with apparatus and uncertainties, clearly demonstrating an understanding of important experimental constraints.
A succinct hypothesis $$v_t^2 \propto T$$ is presented, clearly linking theoretical relationships to expectations.
No calibration procedure is mentioned for the temperature probe; specify how probe accuracy was verified to ensure temperature reliability.
Safety and ethical risks are conflated; separate procedural safety risks from ethical concerns to sharpen methodological evaluation.
While the narrative pivots nicely to viscosity, expanding on broader applications (e.g. industrial fluid dynamics or biochemical flows) would deepen context and strengthen justification.
The notation $$v_t^{\alpha}$$ in Relationship 1 is ambiguous: the exponentâs meaning is unclear and should be specified or corrected.
The student effectively grounds the exploration in a real-world context by linking the research motivation to observations at the 2024 Olympics, enhancing engagement and relevance.
The research question is concise, specifies mass, temperature range and drop conditions, and is directly linked to the investigationâs variables.
Equations are derived clearly by equating weight and drag, demonstrating sound theoretical foundations for terminal velocity.
The assumption of constant drag coefficient is noted but lacks quantitative justification; discuss how this approximation may affect validity.
The video-analysis procedure is described clearly, including the use of calibration marks to obtain velocity data from Tracker.
Suggest adding a quantified estimate of calibration error (e.g. Â± mm) to reflect uncertainty in tracked distances.
Viscosity or density measurement methods are omitted; include a procedure for measuring fluid properties or explain why density calibration is unnecessary.
The method steps are detailed and well-sequenced, enabling reproducibility of apparatus setup and data collection.
The risk assessment addresses both ethical and safety considerations, demonstrating thoughtful experimental planning.
The description omits details of pixel-to-meter calibration and its uncertainty; include how the scale was determined inside Tracker.
Table 4 and Equation 6 clearly present averaging and rangeâmethod uncertainty calculations, demonstrating data processing rigor.
Tables include measured and extrapolated values, showing transparency in processing decisions.
Discuss how missing measured data at higher temperatures (T â¥ 40 Â°C) might bias regression and consider alternative approaches.
Uncertainties are computed for measured averages but not propagated through extrapolated Desmos predictions, omitting systematic uncertainty evaluation.
Notation switches between $$v_r$$ and $$v_t$$ in text and figures; inconsistent terminology may confuse readers.
Ensure consistent use of the terminal velocity symbol (e.g. $$v_t$$) across all equations, tables, and figures for clarity.
Several cells are blank for extrapolated data in Tables 1â3, which hinders clarity on data completeness and reproducibility.
Recommend calculating and reporting uncertainty intervals for extrapolated points (e.g. via bootstrapping or propagation) to strengthen analysis.
The interpretation rightly identifies systematic bias from extrapolated data and links variance to reliability concerns.
The conclusion asserts the hypothesis is supported despite noting a linear trend that contradicts the squared relationship, causing inconsistency.
Uncertainties in regression slope and intercept are not reported; this omission limits assessment of fit precision.
Include confidence intervals or error margins for lineâofâbestâfit parameters to fully communicate data processing accuracy.
Figures 4 and 5 are wellâlabelled with clear captions and error bars, supporting effective visual communication.
Refine the conclusion to align fully with the data analysis, explicitly stating how results support or refute the original hypothesis.
The evaluation identifies specific procedural limitations and discusses their qualitative impact, demonstrating critical reflection.
Comparative discussion of results with Stokeâs law would enrich the scientific context and demonstrate deeper connection to established models.
Quantitative impact of refraction or frame-rate limitations on measured velocity is not estimated; this reduces depth of evaluation.
References lack consistent formatting (e.g. inclusion of DOIs, uniform citation style); standardize entries to ensure academic rigor.
Recommend quantifying the magnitude of refraction error by testing a control object through the same beaker walls.
Future studies could specify required camera fps relative to expected speed to ensure higher-temperature trials are fully captured.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
